hook: A Roman bathhouse found beneath a city car park, left open to wander.
facts: In 1971, construction works between via Lecco and via Dante exposed eight rooms of a large Roman building over about 1,500 square metres.
From 2006 the remains were fully excavated while a multi-storey car park was built over them.
Dated to two phases, the later 1st century AD and around the mid-2nd century, and read as a thermal bath complex.
The archaeological area opened on the ground floor of the Valduce car park in January 2011, with walkways and panels.
w: Digging foundations for a multi-storey car park in 1971, builders cut into eight rooms of a Roman bathhouse, 1,500 square metres of it. Rather than bury the find, Como roofed the car park over the ruins and let visitors walk among the brick pillars. Antiquity in the parking garage.

hook: A domed temple on the lakefront full of the battery inventor's relics.
facts: Neoclassical museum honouring Alessandro Volta, born and died in Como, inventor of the electric battery.
Built in 1927 for the centenary of his death, funded by industrialist Francesco Somaini and donated to the city.
Pantheon-inspired domed rotunda on the lakeside promenade.
Many original instruments were destroyed in an 1899 fire; the museum shows surviving originals and reconstructions.
w: Como's most famous son gave the world the battery, and this domed neoclassical temple, raised in 1927 for the centenary of his death, keeps his instruments and relics. Fire destroyed many of Volta's originals back in 1899, so reconstructions fill the gaps. A pantheon to the man behind every AA cell.

hook: Nine hundred stone steps up an old smugglers' path to the Swiss border.
facts: A flight of about 900 stone steps in the Parco Regionale Spina Verde, on a loop from the Via Stefano Franscini car park.
Historically part of routes used by smugglers carrying watches, cigarettes and chocolate from Switzerland after the war.
Gradients reach about 40 percent; a border net is visible during the climb.
The Scala del Paradiso name is a modern popular label for this stepped stretch.
w: Nine hundred stone steps climb the Spina Verde ridge toward the Swiss border, on paths once trodden by smugglers hauling watches and chocolate after the war. Gradients hit 40 percent; a border net still flashes through the trees. They called it the stairway to paradise. Earn the view the hard way.

hook: A walkway bolted into a gorge, guarded by a frescoed Devil's House.
facts: A gorge carved by the River Pioverna, with metal walkways clinging to the rock over the torrent; Stendhal praised it.
At its entrance stands the Ca' del Diavol, the Devil's House, named for upper-storey frescoes of zodiac signs and devil figures.
The tower now holds a small multimedia museum on the gorge and Bellano's history.
legend: Folklore tells of satanic rites, a buried warrior with hidden treasure, and secret tunnels under the old town during Austrian rule.
w: A metal walkway bolts you to the rock above the roaring Pioverna, deep in a gorge Stendhal adored. At its mouth squats the Ca' del Diavol, the Devil's House, its upper rooms frescoed with zodiac signs and demons. Legend whispers of satanic rites and buried treasure. Vertigo and folklore, together.

hook: A medieval keep above Varenna, haunted by ghosts in white plaster.
facts: Medieval hilltop fortification overlooking Varenna and the lake, with a square keep and falconry displays.
The white ghost figures are a modern art installation, plaster cast over volunteers and renewed each year.
Reached on foot up from Varenna or from the hamlet of Vezio.
legend: Tradition credits its founding to the Lombard queen Theodelinda, whose ghost is said to wander the grounds.
w: Above Varenna a square medieval keep watches the lake, said in legend to have been raised by the Lombard queen Theodelinda, whose ghost still walks the grounds. Stranger are the white plaster figures posed about the place, cast over volunteers each year. Falconry, phantoms and a knockout view.

hook: Italy's shortest river: milk-white, here in spring, gone by autumn.
facts: About 250 metres long, one of the shortest rivers in Italy, with milky-white foaming water that gives it its name.
Intermittent: it typically dries in mid-October and reappears in the second half of March.
Leonardo da Vinci studied it and mentioned it in the Codex Atlanticus as the shortest river known.
The intermittency points to an underground karst basin in the Grigna massif above.
w: Barely 250 metres long, one of Italy's shortest rivers froths milk-white from a cave in the Grigna and tumbles to the lake, then vanishes each October to return in March. Leonardo da Vinci came to puzzle over it and noted it in his Codex. A river that keeps its own mysterious calendar.

hook: The lake's only island, burned in 1169 and cursed ever after.
facts: The only island in Lake Como, sacked and burned on 24 June 1169 by Como and its ally Frederick Barbarossa.
Survivors fled to Varenna; the island's churches, including the basilica of Sant'Eufemia, were destroyed.
It remains near-empty, dotted with church ruins, now an archaeological park.
The annual Festa di San Giovanni each 24 June re-stages the burning with fireworks.
legend: In 1175 the Bishop of Como is said to have cursed it: no bells, no stone on stone, no innkeeper to survive.
w: Lake Como's only island once held a thriving town, until Como and Barbarossa burned it in 1169 and a bishop, they say, cursed the ruins: no bells, no inn, no stone on stone. Eight centuries on it stays near-empty, dotted with church ruins. Each June fireworks re-stage the burning. A scorched paradise.

hook: A Romanesque priory and 13th-century cloister in profound lakeside hush.
facts: Religious complex on the Olgiasca promontory at the lake's northern tip; an earlier church of Santa Giustina was founded in the 7th century.
The monastery was consecrated in 1138; first recorded as San Nicolo in 1169.
Its celebrated cloister, built 1252 to 1257, blends round Romanesque arches with the first pointed Gothic.
Since 1938 Cistercian monks have lived here, producing herbal liqueurs.
w: On a quiet promontory at the lake's northern tip, Cistercian monks still live around a Romanesque priory whose jewel, a cloister of 1252, marries round arches with the first pointed Gothic. They distil herbal liqueurs in the old way. Behind it lie the apse ruins of a 7th-century church. Otherworldly hush.

hook: A bulldozed hill village rebuilt as a Las Vegas dream, now a ruin.
facts: Built in the 1960s and 70s by entrepreneur Mario Bagno, who bought and demolished the old farming village to raise a Las Vegas-style City of Toys.
It featured a pagoda and minaret, a ballroom, a zoo, restaurants and a hotel.
A 1976 landslide cut the access road and accelerated its abandonment.
It is now a decayed, graffiti-covered ruin and a minor tourist curiosity.
w: In the 1960s a building tycoon bulldozed a hill village to raise his own Las Vegas, a City of Toys with a pagoda, a ballroom and a zoo. A 1976 landslide severed the road and the dream rotted where it stood. Now graffiti and weeds claim the minaret. A folly of concrete and hubris, returning to the hill.

hook: Twin streams plunge into the lake beneath an arched stone bridge.
facts: At Nesso two streams meet and plunge about 200 metres into the lake through a slot in the rock.
The Ponte della Civera, a humpbacked stone bridge of Roman foundation, frames the classic view of the falls.
Reached from the village square by a long stone staircase of around 340 steps.
It lies on the Triangolo Lariano shore, about 20 minutes south of Bellagio.
w: In the sleepy village of Nesso two streams meet and plunge 200 metres into the lake through a slot in the rock, spanned by a humpbacked stone bridge of Roman footing. Some 340 steps drop from the square to the famous view. Water, stone and gravity, in one perfect frame. Worth every knee-jarring step.

hook: WWI trenches against an invasion that never came, high above Cernobbio.
facts: A WWI defensive line built in 1915 and 1916 along the Swiss border, for fear of an attack through neutral Switzerland.
It comprised about 72 km of trenches, 88 artillery positions and hundreds of kilometres of mule paths.
It never saw combat; the feared northern offensive never materialised.
The Monte Bisbino battery sector was restored by the Como Alpini and opened to the public in 2011.
w: High on Monte Bisbino above Cernobbio run trenches and gun positions Italy carved in 1915, terrified of an attack through neutral Switzerland that never came. Seventy kilometres of trench, hundreds of miles of mule track, not a shot fired. The Alpini restored this stretch. Walk a war that was only imagined.

hook: The 'Balcony of Italy' on the Swiss border, all of Lake Lugano below.
facts: A panoramic terrace on Monte Sighignola at about 1,320 metres, above Lanzo d'Intelvi.
It earns the Balcony of Italy name for sweeping views over Lake Lugano and the city of Lugano.
The border runs across the summit, but the belvedere itself sits on the Italian side.
A cable car once climbed to the summit from the Swiss side but is long defunct.
w: They call this border-straddling terrace at 1,320 metres the Balcony of Italy, and it earns the name: the whole of Lake Lugano and the Alps unfurl below. The summit line marks the Swiss frontier; a cable car once hauled visitors up from the far side. Drive the Intelvi hairpins and gasp.

hook: A bench built for giants, framing the Lecco branch and the Grigne.
facts: Part of the Big Bench Community Project begun by car designer Chris Bangle; this is bench number 137.
Yellow-and-red two-tone bench in a meadow, inaugurated in May 2021.
It overlooks the Lecco branch of the lake and the jagged Grigne peaks.
A short walk from the Cineteatro Grigne parking in Civenna, a frazione of Bellagio.
w: Perched in a meadow above Civenna sits a bench built for giants, painted yellow and red, where grown adults dangle their legs like toddlers. Number 137 of an art project dreamed up by a car designer, it frames the Lecco branch and the jagged Grigne. Climb up and feel pleasantly small.

hook: A medieval 'village of a thousand steps' with barely twenty residents.
facts: A fortified medieval hamlet built around a cobbled square on a rocky lake promontory, known as the village of a thousand steps.
The castle was built around 1363 to 1370 on Roman foundations, with a square 11th-century tower.
Rare external arched tombs of the Andreani counts stand beside the church of San Tommaso di Canterbury.
Rock-carved staircases drop to an old pier; only about 15 to 20 people still live here.
w: They call it the village of a thousand steps, and the rock-cut stairs of Corenno Plinio drop in cobbled flights to an old pier. A 14th-century castle crowns the promontory; beside the church wait rare open-air tombs of the Andreani counts. Fewer than twenty people still live here. A medieval freeze-frame.

hook: Lecco's free pebble beach, a local secret minutes from the station.
facts: A free-access pebble beach commonly known as Lecco's beach, about five minutes from Lecco station.
It has a steep-shelving pebble cove and a grassy area with a kids' play zone; free parking and a food court, no sunbeds.
The water deepens quickly off the pebbles.
It sits beside the Orsa Maggiore beach club, on the Lecco branch shore.
w: No resort, no sunbeds, just a free pebble shore the people of Lecco keep mostly to themselves, minutes from the station. The stones shelve fast into deep, clear water; a grassy patch and a kids' play area sit behind. Come on a summer weekend and join the locals. The lake without the price tag.

hook: A near-vertical cable car to a hill village the crowds never reach.
facts: A hilltop village at about 860 metres, reached by the Argegno to Pigra cable car, inaugurated in 1971 and overhauled in 2011.
The cableway climbs 653 metres in about five minutes at gradients up to 71 percent, one of the steepest public cableways around.
The village square and the church of Santa Margherita give a terrace view over the lake.
It is operated by ATM, Milan's transit authority.
w: A near-vertical cable car hauls you 650 metres out of Argegno in five breathless minutes, climbing a 71 percent gradient to the hill village of Pigra. Up top, the square and the little church of Santa Margherita open onto a terrace view of the lake. Most crowds never find it. Ride the wire, escape the shore.

hook: A maze of lakeside alleys where houses rise straight from the water.
facts: A small comune on the western shore between Argegno and Moltrasio.
Its historic centre is a maze of carrugi, narrow stone alleys, vaulted passages and staircases descending to the water.
Houses are built directly onto the lake, in a medieval defensive layout.
It is regularly listed among Lake Como's most picturesque small villages.
w: Wedged between cliff and water on the quiet western shore, Brienno keeps a tangle of carrugi, vaulted alleys and stairways tumbling to houses that rise straight from the lake. No grand villa, no crowd, just a medieval village going about its day. Wander slowly and let it close around you.

hook: Where Mussolini was caught hiding in a convoy, in 1945.
facts: Housed in Palazzo Manzi, an early-19th-century Napoleonic-era palace on the Dongo waterfront.
Mussolini and RSI ministers were captured at a partisan roadblock between Musso and Dongo on 27 April 1945.
The captives were brought to Palazzo Manzi to formalise the arrest; Mussolini was shot the next day down the shore.
The redesigned museum opened in April 2014, with seven rooms on the Resistance and the capture.
w: In a Napoleonic-era palazzo on the Dongo waterfront, this museum tells of the day in April 1945 when partisans stopped a German convoy and found Mussolini hiding under a soldier's greatcoat. Caught here, he was shot the next day down the shore. Seven rooms on the war's last act, where it actually ended.

hook: A lone tower above Como, fortified for Barbarossa in 1158.
facts: A military fortification on a hill about 430 metres above Como; the surviving square tower is nearly 30 metres high.
Restored and fortified with Frederick Barbarossa's support in 1158, during his war with the Lombard League.
Earlier it was one of the last Byzantine strongholds, surrendering to the Lombards in 588.
Now a symbol of the Parco Spina Verde.
w: A lone medieval tower nearly 30 metres tall crowns a hill above Como, fortified for Barbarossa in 1158 during his war with the Lombard cities. Earlier it held out as one of the last Byzantine strongholds. Climb through the Spina Verde woods for the long view down the lake. A stub of empire on a green hill.

hook: A five-naved Romanesque basilica, older and quieter than the cathedral.
facts: A Romanesque basilica built 1050 to 1085 by Benedictine monks on a 5th-century early-Christian church.
Consecrated in 1095 by Pope Urban II; built of grey Moltrasio stone with five naves and two tall bell towers.
The apse holds a notable mid-14th-century fresco cycle.
Bought by the city of Como in 1974, it now hosts the University of Insubria's law faculty.
w: Five soaring naves and two tall bell towers mark this grey-stone Romanesque basilica, built by Benedictine monks between 1050 and 1085 and consecrated by a pope in 1095. Behind the altar glows a 14th-century fresco cycle. Quieter than Como's cathedral, and far older. Step in for the stone and the silence.

hook: One of Europe's finest late-Baroque churches, high up a mountain valley.
facts: In the hamlet of Scaria, built in the 15th century and wholly remodelled around 1709 to 1752.
Remade by the Scaria-born Carloni brothers: Carlo Innocenzo painted the frescoes, Diego Francesco moulded the stucco.
Considered one of the best examples of late Baroque in Europe for its stylistic unity.
The Carloni were major Rococo artists active across the continent; Scaria is the valley's capital of sacred art.
w: High in an Intelvi hamlet stands one of Europe's finest late-Baroque churches, remade between 1709 and 1752 by the Carloni brothers, locals who became star artists across the continent. One painted the frescoes, the other moulded the stucco, in dizzying unity. Pure Rococo theatre, far up a mountain valley.
